title: Advanced Logic & AI Ethics
description: Chuyên đề tổng hợp: Logic, xác suất nâng cao, Python thực chiến, AI systems, và Responsible AI.

questions.prompt: If P(A) = 0.4, P(B) = 0.5, and P(A union B) = 0.7, what is P(A intersection B)?
questions.explanation: P(A union B) = P(A) + P(B) - P(A intersect B) => 0.7 = 0.4 + 0.5 - x => x = 0.2

questions.prompt: A fair die is rolled. What is the probability that the result is prime given that it is odd?
questions.explanation: Odd numbers on a die: {1,3,5}. Prime among those: {3,5}. P = 2/3.

questions.prompt: Which logical statement is the contrapositive of "If it rains, then the ground is wet"?

options.text: If the ground is wet, then it rains.
If it does not rain, then the ground is not wet.
If the ground is not wet, then it does not rain.
The ground is wet only if it rains.

questions.explanation: Contrapositive of P->Q is NOT Q -> NOT P. So "not wet -> not rain".

questions.prompt: What is the expected number of coin flips needed to get two consecutive heads?
questions.explanation: This is a classic Markov chain problem. E[T] = 6 for two consecutive heads with a fair coin.

questions.prompt: A 3x3 matrix has eigenvalues 1, 2, 3. What is its determinant?
questions.explanation: Determinant = product of eigenvalues = 1 x 2 x 3 = 6.

questions.prompt: How many distinct ways can 5 students be arranged in a row if 2 specific students must not be adjacent?
questions.explanation: Total arrangements = 5! = 120. Arrangements where the 2 ARE adjacent = 4! x 2 = 48. Answer = 120 - 48 = 72.

questions.prompt: Which of the following is a tautology?
questions.explanation: P OR NOT P is always true regardless of truth value of P - this is the law of excluded middle.

questions.prompt: Given a normal distribution with mean 50 and std 10, what percentage of data falls between 40 and 60?
questions.explanation: 40 and 60 are exactly 1 standard deviation from the mean. By the 68-95-99.7 rule, ~68% of data falls within 1 SD.

questions.prompt: Which statement about logical equivalence is correct?

options.text: P -> Q is equivalent to Q -> P
P -> Q is equivalent to NOT P OR Q
P -> Q is equivalent to P AND NOT Q
NOT(P -> Q) is equivalent to NOT P AND NOT Q

questions.explanation: P -> Q (if P then Q) is logically equivalent to NOT P OR Q.

questions.prompt: In a Bayesian network, if A causes B and B causes C, and A and C are conditionally independent given B, this is called:
questions.explanation: A->B->C is a serial connection (causal chain). A and C are d-separated given B.

questions.prompt:
What does the following Python expression evaluate to?

all(x % 2 == 0 for x in [2, 4, 6, 8])
questions.explanation: all() returns True if every element satisfies the condition. All are even, so True.

questions.prompt: Which Python code correctly implements a memoized Fibonacci using functools?

options.text:
@cache
def fib(n): return n if n<2 else fib(n-1)+fib(n-2)

@lru_cache(None)
def fib(n): return n if n<2 else fib(n-1)+fib(n-2)
Both A and C are correct

questions.explanation: Both @cache (Python 3.9+) and @lru_cache(None) (maxsize=None) implement memoization.

questions.prompt:
What is the output of this Python code?

x = [1,2,3]
y = x
y.append(4)
print(len(x))
questions.explanation: y = x makes y a reference to the same list object. Appending to y also modifies x. len(x) = 4.

questions.prompt: Which of the following correctly filters a pandas DataFrame df to rows where column "score" is in the top 25%?
questions.explanation: quantile(0.75) gives the 75th percentile value. Filtering >= that captures the top 25%.

questions.prompt: What does "yield from iterable" do in a Python generator?

options.text: Yields the entire iterable as a single item
Yields each item from the iterable one at a time

questions.explanation: "yield from" delegates to a sub-iterable, yielding each element individually.

questions.prompt: Given numpy array a = np.array([[1,2],[3,4]]), what does np.trace(a) return?
questions.explanation: np.trace returns the sum of diagonal elements: 1 + 4 = 5.

questions.prompt: Which Python pattern correctly implements a Singleton class?
questions.explanation: Singleton overrides __new__ to return the same instance every time.

questions.prompt: What is the result of: sorted(["banana","apple","cherry"], key=lambda x: x[-1])?
questions.explanation: Last chars: apple->e, banana->a, cherry->y. Sorted: a<e<y => banana, apple, cherry.

questions.prompt: What does collections.defaultdict(list) do when you access a missing key?

options.text: Creates an empty list for that key

questions.explanation: defaultdict(list) auto-creates an empty list [] for any missing key accessed.

questions.prompt: Which pandas method stacks a wide DataFrame (multiple columns) into a long format (two columns: variable, value)?
questions.explanation: pd.melt() unpivots a DataFrame from wide to long format.

questions.prompt: What is the time complexity of Python dict lookup on average?
questions.explanation: Python dict uses a hash table. Average case lookup is O(1).

questions.prompt:
What does the following return?

import numpy as np
np.argmax(np.array([3,1,4,1,5,9,2,6]))
questions.explanation: np.argmax returns the index of the maximum value. Max is 9 at index 5.

questions.prompt: Which statement about Python context managers is TRUE?

options.text: __exit__ handles exceptions if it returns True
__exit__ is only called on normal exit

questions.explanation: If __exit__ returns True it suppresses the exception. It is called regardless of whether an exception occurred.

questions.prompt: In sklearn, what does cross_val_score(model, X, y, cv=5) return?

options.text: An array of 5 scores, one per fold

questions.explanation: cross_val_score returns an array of scores, one for each of the cv=5 folds.

questions.prompt: What is the output of: [x**2 for x in range(5) if x % 2 != 0]?
questions.explanation: Odd x in range(5): 1,3. Their squares: 1, 9. Result: [1,9].

questions.prompt: In reinforcement learning, the Bellman equation expresses the value of a state as:

options.text: Immediate reward plus discounted value of next state

questions.explanation: The Bellman equation: V(s) = R(s) + gamma * max_a V(s'). It combines immediate reward and discounted future value.

questions.prompt: Which technique allows a neural network to focus on relevant parts of the input sequence during generation?
questions.explanation: Attention mechanisms compute weighted sums over input positions, allowing the model to focus on relevant context.

questions.prompt: What is "catastrophic forgetting" in neural networks?

options.text: Loss of previously learned knowledge when trained on new data

questions.explanation: Catastrophic forgetting occurs in continual learning: training on new tasks destroys performance on old ones.

questions.prompt: In the context of AI products, what does "cold start problem" refer to?

options.text: Models taking too long to initialize
Difficulty making recommendations for new users/items with no history

questions.explanation: Cold start: recommender systems cannot make good predictions for new users or items lacking interaction history.

questions.prompt: What is the primary purpose of a Feature Store in MLOps?

options.text: To serve consistent, pre-computed features for both training and serving

questions.explanation: Feature stores ensure consistent feature computation between training and serving, preventing training-serving skew.

questions.prompt: Which metric is most appropriate for evaluating a model on a severely imbalanced dataset (e.g., 99% negative)?

options.text: Both B and C are better than A

questions.explanation: Accuracy is misleading with imbalanced data (a model predicting all negative gets 99%). F1 and AUC-ROC are more informative.

questions.prompt: In NLP, what does "subword tokenization" (e.g., BPE) help with?

options.text: Handling out-of-vocabulary words and morphologically rich languages

questions.explanation: BPE and other subword methods split rare words into known subword units, handling OOV words and rich morphology.

questions.prompt: What distinguishes a "foundation model" from a traditional ML model?

options.text: Foundation models use only supervised learning
Foundation models are pre-trained at scale and adapted to many downstream tasks
Foundation models only work with text data

questions.explanation: Foundation models (e.g., GPT, BERT) are large-scale pre-trained models adapted via fine-tuning or prompting to many tasks.

questions.prompt: Which approach best mitigates hallucination in LLM-based question answering?
questions.explanation: RAG grounds responses in retrieved factual documents, significantly reducing hallucinations.

questions.prompt: In product analytics, "DAU/MAU ratio" measures:

options.text: Daily revenue per monthly active user
User engagement/stickiness (how often monthly users return daily)
Daily acquisition rate vs monthly churn

questions.explanation: DAU/MAU ratio (stickiness) indicates how often monthly users engage daily. Higher ratio = more engaged product.

questions.prompt: What is "model distillation"?

options.text: Training a small model to mimic the behavior of a larger model
Converting a model to a different framework

questions.explanation: Knowledge distillation trains a smaller "student" model to replicate the outputs of a larger "teacher" model.

questions.prompt: Which regularization technique randomly sets neurons to zero during training?
questions.explanation: Dropout randomly zeros out neurons during training to prevent co-adaptation and reduce overfitting.

questions.prompt: In the context of search and ranking, what does NDCG measure?

options.text: Quality of ranking considering both relevance and position

questions.explanation: Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (NDCG) measures ranking quality - highly relevant items should appear at top positions.

questions.prompt: What is "prompt injection" in LLM security?

options.text: Malicious input that overrides system instructions to manipulate LLM behavior
Adding too many tokens to a prompt
Using prompts to improve model accuracy

questions.explanation: Prompt injection attacks embed instructions in user input to override system prompts and hijack LLM behavior.

questions.prompt: In a recommendation system, collaborative filtering assumes:

options.text: Users with similar content preferences are similar
Users who behave similarly will like similar items
Items with similar features should be recommended together
All users have the same preferences

questions.explanation: Collaborative filtering uses user-item interaction patterns (behavior similarity) rather than item content features.

questions.prompt: What is the vanishing gradient problem?

options.text: Gradients becoming too large during backpropagation
Gradients becoming extremely small in deep networks making early layers learn very slowly

questions.explanation: In deep networks, gradients can shrink exponentially during backpropagation through many layers, slowing learning.

questions.prompt: Which AI deployment strategy routes a small percentage of live traffic to a new model while the old model handles the rest?
questions.explanation: Canary deployment routes a small traffic slice (e.g. 5%) to the new model to test it safely before full rollout.

questions.prompt: What is "transfer learning"?

options.text: Moving a model from one server to another
Using knowledge from a pre-trained model as a starting point for a new task
Transferring data between training and serving pipelines
Using multiple models in an ensemble

questions.explanation: Transfer learning leverages representations learned on one task (e.g., ImageNet classification) for a different task.

questions.prompt: In multi-armed bandit problems, the exploration-exploitation tradeoff refers to:

options.text: Choosing between fast and slow inference
Balancing trying new options vs. using known best options
Balancing training data size vs. model complexity
Choosing between batch and online learning

questions.explanation: Exploration tries new arms to gather information; exploitation uses known best arm. Balancing both is the key challenge.

questions.prompt: What does "data augmentation" accomplish in deep learning?

options.text: Artificially increases training set size by creating modified copies of existing data
Downloads more training data from the internet

questions.explanation: Data augmentation (flips, crops, rotations, color jitter) creates varied training examples to reduce overfitting.

questions.prompt: Which technique is used to explain individual predictions of a black-box model?
questions.explanation: SHAP (SHapley Additive exPlanations) attributes each feature's contribution to a specific prediction.

questions.prompt: What is "concept drift" in deployed ML models?

options.text: The statistical properties of the target variable change over time, degrading model performance

questions.explanation: Concept drift: real-world data distributions shift over time (e.g. user behavior changes), degrading a trained model.

questions.prompt: In NLP, ROUGE score is primarily used to evaluate:
questions.explanation: ROUGE (Recall-Oriented Understudy for Gisting Evaluation) measures overlap between generated and reference summaries.

questions.prompt: What distinguishes fine-tuning from few-shot prompting?

options.text: Fine-tuning updates model weights; few-shot prompting does not
Few-shot prompting is always more accurate
Fine-tuning only works for classification tasks

questions.explanation: Fine-tuning: model weights are updated on task-specific data. Few-shot: examples in the prompt only, no weight updates.

questions.prompt: What is the purpose of a "validation set" (distinct from test set)?

options.text: Tuning hyperparameters and model selection during development
Same purpose as test set, just different name

questions.explanation: Validation set tunes hyperparameters/model selection. Test set provides unbiased final evaluation (used only once).

questions.prompt: A system decides loan eligibility. It has 90% accuracy for majority group but only 70% for minority group. This is an example of:
questions.explanation: Significant performance disparity across demographic groups indicates algorithmic bias, which requires mitigation.

questions.prompt: What logical fallacy is committed here: "We cannot trust AI systems because AI researcher Dr. X was arrested for fraud"?
questions.explanation: Ad hominem attacks the person rather than the argument. The researcher's conduct is irrelevant to AI system trustworthiness.

questions.prompt: The principle that AI systems should not cause harm and should protect user wellbeing is known as:
questions.explanation: Non-maleficence (do no harm) is a core AI ethics principle requiring systems to avoid causing harm to users or society.

questions.prompt: Which statement is logically equivalent to "Not all birds can fly"?
questions.explanation: "Not all birds can fly" means "there exists at least one bird that cannot fly" = "Some birds cannot fly".

questions.prompt: An AI hiring tool is found to favor candidates from elite universities. Before deployment, this should be addressed through:

options.text: Bias audit and fairness testing before deployment
Adding a disclaimer to the tool
Reducing the weight of university features

questions.explanation: Responsible AI requires proactive bias auditing and fairness testing before deployment, not reactive monitoring only.

questions.prompt: What does "right to explanation" under GDPR Article 22 require?

options.text: All AI systems must be open source
Individuals have the right to meaningful explanation of automated decisions affecting them
Companies must publish all training data

questions.explanation: GDPR Article 22 grants individuals the right to obtain meaningful explanation for automated decisions affecting them.

questions.prompt:
Which is the correct chain of reasoning?
Premise 1: All supervised learning models require labeled data.
Premise 2: Random forest is a supervised learning model.
Conclusion: ?

options.text: Random forest does not need data
Labeled data is not needed for supervised learning

questions.explanation: Universal syllogism: All A are B, C is A, therefore C is B. Random forest is supervised, so it requires labeled data.

questions.prompt: The concept that AI developers should be able to answer for AI system decisions and outcomes is called:
questions.explanation: Accountability means AI developers and deployers can be held responsible for the behavior and outcomes of their systems.

questions.prompt: An AI system performs well in testing but fails on real users from different demographics. This is likely due to:
questions.explanation: When test data does not represent real-world demographics, models fail in deployment due to distribution shift or dataset bias.

questions.prompt: Which of the following best describes "dual-use" concern in AI?

options.text: Using two different AI models for the same task
AI technology developed for beneficial purposes can also be weaponized or misused
Deploying models in two geographic regions simultaneously

questions.explanation: Dual-use: AI capabilities (e.g., deepfakes, autonomous systems, biodesign) can be used for both beneficial and harmful purposes.
